did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep transfer learning and ensemble learning methods for melanoma classification. Specifically, it employs pre-trained deep learning models (VGG, ResNet, InceptionResNet, MobileNet) for feature extraction and ensemble models (EfficientNet, Xception, DenseNet) for classification on ISIC 2017-2020 dermatology datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a novel ensemble deep transfer learning model for melanoma classification. It details specific methodologies including data preprocessing, feature extraction using VGG, ResNet, InceptionResNet, and MobileNet, and classification using EfficientNet, Xception, and DenseNet. The study reports original quantitative results (accuracy scores of 96.10% to 98.60%) on ISIC 2017-2020 datasets, distinguishing it from a review paper despite containing a literature survey section.

title: Melanoma classification using ensemble deep transfer learning
journal: IAES International Journal of Artificial Intelligence
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early melanoma detection by proposing an ensemble deep transfer learning model to improve classification accuracy. The researchers utilized publicly available ISIC 2017–2020 datasets, performing preprocessing steps such as normalization and label encoding to handle data imbalance and noise. Feature extraction was conducted using pre-trained models including VGG, ResNet, InceptionResNet, and MobileNet, with the resulting vectors fused for comprehensive representation. Classification was then performed using an ensemble of EfficientNet, Xception, and DenseNet models trained on the fused features. The proposed method achieved average accuracy scores ranging from 96.10% to 98.60% across the datasets, demonstrating superior performance compared to individual transfer learning models. These results indicate that the ensemble approach effectively enhances the reliability and precision of automated melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for classification in the proposed ensemble deep transfer learning model. The study focuses on distinguishing melanoma from other skin lesions using ISIC 2017-2020 datasets and a Kaggle ISIC dataset containing 1497 malignant mole photos.
One of the four class groups present in the ISIC 2017 dataset ground truth, used as a comparator to melanoma in the multi-class context of that specific dataset.
One of the four class groups present in the ISIC 2017 dataset ground truth, used as a comparator to melanoma.

Listed as a class group in the ISIC 2017 dataset ground truth. Note: The paper uses the term 'melanoma nevus' which likely refers to melanocytic nevi or a specific subset, but it is listed distinctly from 'nevus' in the text.
Used in the Kaggle ISIC dataset (1800 benign photos) and ISIC 2020 dataset metadata (benign or malignant status) as the negative class for binary classification tasks.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma vs. Benign/Malignant status or specific lesion classes (melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, nevus, melanoma nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'melanoma classification' using ensemble deep transfer learning. It predicts discrete diagnostic categories: distinguishing melanoma from benign lesions (binary classification on Kaggle/ISIC 2020) and multi-class classification among four groups (melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, nevus, melanoma nevus) on ISIC 2017. Performance is measured using accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measure on these discrete classes.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights used for feature extraction models (VGG, ResNet, InceptionResNet, MobileNet) and classification models (EfficientNetB0, Xception, DenseNet121). Specific pretraining datasets (e.g., ImageNet) are not explicitly named in the provided text, but 'pre-trained' implies standard ImageNet weights.

architecture_details.feature_fusion: Concatenation of feature vectors from the four backbones followed by normalization

classification_ensemble.details: The concatenated feature vector is processed through EfficientNetB0, Xception, and DenseNet121 to obtain classification probability vectors.

training_details.epochs: 15 epochs (highest accuracy reported at the 15th epoch)

classification_or_output_head.final_output: Classification based on the concatenation of initial probability vectors from the ensemble models

Data augmentation to address data imbalance
Label encoding to make data compatible with deep learning modules

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to address data imbalance issues in the melanoma dataset. Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation angles, flip probabilities) were not reported in the text.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble deep transfer learning model, which fuses features from VGG, ResNet, InceptionResNet, and MobileNet for classification via an ensemble of EfficientNet, Xception, and DenseNet, effectively classifies melanoma images.
The method achieved high accuracy scores on publicly available ISIC datasets: 96.10% on ISIC 2016, 97.23% on ISIC 2017, 97.50% on ISIC 2018, 98.33% on ISIC 2019, and 98.60% on ISIC 2020.

On the Kaggle ISIC dataset, the proposed model achieved an accuracy of 98.55% (with detailed evaluation showing 98.76% accuracy and 0.987 AUC), demonstrating superior performance compared to individual transfer learning models and other baseline methods.

The ensemble approach captures complex patterns and subtle distinctions within melanoma images, resulting in improved classification accuracy and overall system performance compared to conventional parametric methods and single deep learning models.

Deep learning methods, particularly those utilizing transfer learning and ensemble strategies, offer a viable solution to overcome the challenges of inter-class homogeneity and intra-class heterogeneity in skin lesion classification.
important_remarks: The paper reports a discrepancy in the Kaggle ISIC results: the text states an overall accuracy of 95.76%, while Table 4 lists the proposed model's accuracy as 98.76% and the abstract/section 4.1 notes 98.55% for the stacking ensemble; this inconsistency requires clarification.

Training and validation loss metrics indicate peak performance at the 9th epoch (Figure 6a), whereas peak accuracy is reported at the 15th epoch (Figure 6b and text), suggesting potential overfitting or conflicting stopping criteria not fully explained.

The study relies exclusively on publicly available ISIC datasets (2016-2020) and a Kaggle-derived dataset; there is no external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting the assessment of real-world generalizability and robustness to distribution shifts.
Key hyperparameters such as the optimizer, learning rate, and batch size are not reported, hindering reproducibility of the deep transfer learning experiments.
The dataset descriptions note the presence of 'sounds and artefacts' in the Kaggle ISIC images, indicating potential data quality issues that may affect model performance and clinical applicability.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the comparative results, making it difficult to assess if the performance gains over baselines are statistically significant.

The model architecture involves fusing features from four backbones (VGG, ResNet, InceptionResNet, MobileNet) and classifying with an ensemble of three models (EfficientNet, Xception, DenseNet); the computational cost and inference latency for such a complex ensemble are not discussed, posing a barrier to clinical deployment.

The study addresses data imbalance via augmentation but does not specify the techniques used or quantify the class distribution before and after augmentation, which is critical for evaluating bias mitigation.
